text: BOWDEN, a parish in Roxburghshire, about 6 m. in length, and 4\frac{1}{2} m. in breadth. Pop. in 1801, 829; in 1841, 857. The v. of B. is 3 m. from Melrose.
BOWDEN-EDGE, a township in the p. of Chapel-en-le-Frith, in Derbyshire. Pop. 1,021.
BOWDEN (LITTLE), a parish in Northamptonshire, 1 m. SE of Market-Harborough. Area 2,410 acres. Pop. 435.
BOWDEN (Magna), a parish in Leicestershire, 13 m. NE of Market-Harborough, which forms a chapelry to it. Area, inclusive of the chapelry, 3,120 acres. Pop., with chapelry, 3,698.
BOWDOIN, a township in Lincoln co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 17 m. W of Wiscasset. Pop. 2,073.
BOWDOINHAM, a township in Lincoln co., in the state of Maine, U. S., 21 m. S by W of Augusta, on the W side of Kennebec river. Pop. 2,402.
BOWDON, a large parish in Cheshire, 6 m. N by E of Nether Knutsford. Area 18,660 acres. Pop. 9,373.
BOWEN, a village in Madison co., in the state of Arkansas. Pop. 453.
BOWEN'S ISLE, a small island off the E coast of Australia, at the entrance of Jervis bay, in N lat. 35° 6′.
BOWEN (CAPE), a promontory on the W side of Baffin's bay, to the SE of Ponds bay, in N lat. 71° 22', W long. 74° 30'.

BOWEN (PORT), a small bay of N America, on the E side of Prince Regent's inlet, in N lat. 80° .- Also an indentation of the NE coast of Australia. between Entrance island on the N, and Cape Clinton on the S, in N lat. 22° 30′, E long. 150° 50′.
BOWER, a parish in Caithness-shire, between Wick and Thurso, about 7 m. in length, by 3 m. in breadth. Pop. in 1801, 1,572; in 1841, 1,689.
BOWER-CHALK, a parish in Wilts, 7½ m. SSW of Wilton. Area 3,120 acres. Pop. 447.
BOWER-HINTON, a hamlet in the p. of Martock, Somersetshire. Pop. with Hurst, 688.
BOWERS-GIFFORD, a parish in Essex, 4 m. WSW of Rayleigh. Area 2,230 acres. Pop. 249.
BOWES, a parish and village in the N. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. SW of Barnard-castle. Area 18,440 acres. Pop. 850.
BOWLAND (LITTLE), a township in Whalley p., in Lancashire. Area, with Leagram, 7,960 acres. Pop. 273.
BOWLAND-FOREST (High and Low), two townships in the W. R. of Yorkshire; the former in Slaidburn p., the latter in Whalley p. Area of High B. 17,800 acres. Pop. 181. Pop. of Low B. 330.
BOWLD, a hamlet in Idbury p., in Oxfordshire. Pop. 59.
BOWLES, a township of Franklin co. U. S., in the state of Missouri. Pop. in 1840, 1,449.
BOWLEY, a township in Bodenham p., in Herefordshire. Pop. 206.
BOWLING, a township in the p. of Bradford, in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 11 m. SW of Bradford. Area 1,240 acres. Pop. 8,918.

BOWLING GREEN, a village, cap. of Caroline co., U. S., in the state of Virginia, 40 m. N of Richmond. Pop. in 1840 about 250.—Also a village, cap. of Warren co., in the state of Kentucky, on the Big Barren river, 180 m. from the Ohio, with which it has steam communication. Pop. in 1840, 1,400. It has a considerable trade, especially in tobacco.—Also a township of Licking co., in the state of Ohio. Pop. in 1840, 1,464.—Also a village, cap. of Clay co., in the state of Iowa, 60 m. SW of Indianapolis, on the Eel river. Pop. in 1840, 235.—Also a township of Pettis co., in the state of Missouri. Pop. in 1840, 410.—Also a township of Marion co., in the state of Ohio, on the S side of the Scioto. Pop. 324.
BOWMORE, a port and village in the island of Islay, on the E side of Lochindaal, 11 m. SSW of Port-Askaig. Pop. 1,500.

BOWNESS, a parish in Cumberland, 13 m. WNW of Carlisle, on the S coast of the Solway frith. It comprises the townships of Anthorn, B., Drumburgh, and Fingland. Area 10,830 acres. Pop. 1,488.— Also a hamlet in the p. of Windermere, Westmoreland, 8 m. WNW of Kirkby-Kendal, finely situated on a small bay on the E shore of Windermere lake. It is a favourite resort of tourists, and the v. has increased considerably within the last few years.
BOWRELL, the highest point of the Mittagong trap range, which traverses Australia Felix in a SE direction, between the Wollondilly and the sea.

BOWYER, a fort of Baldwin co., in the state of Alabama, U. S., 36 m. S of Mobile, at the entrance of the principal pass of Mobile bay. It was taken by the English, after a vigorous defence, on the 8th Feb., 1815.

BOX, a parish in Wilts, 7½ m. SW by W of Chippenham, on the line of the Great Western railroad, in a valley of singular beauty, watered by a rivulet of the same name. Area 4,130 acres. Pop. 2,274. Freestone of an excellent quality, much of which was used in erecting the city of Bath, is wrought in this parish. Box-hill in this p., a large extent of elevated ground composed in great part of solid beds of freestone, lying directly between, and almost equidistant from, Chippenham and Bath, presented one of the most formidable obstacles with which the Great Western railway had to contend. A tunnel through this immense mass, 3,128 yards in length, 30 ft. in width, and from 25 to 40 ft. in height, was commenced in 1836, and finished in 1841.
BOXBERG, a town of Baden, 18 m. SSE of Wertheim. Pop. 1,212.
BOXBOROUGH, a township of Middlesex co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., between the Concord and Nashna rivers, 28 m. WNW of Boston. Hops form the chief article of local culture.
BOXFORD, a parish in Berks, 41 m. NW of Newbury. Area 2,880 acres. Pop. with Westbrook 612. BOY -Also a p. in Suffolk, 15 m. W by S of Ipswich. Area 2,450 acres. Pop. 1,121.
BOXFORD, a township of Essex co., in the state of Massachusetts, U.S., 29 m. NE of Boston. Pop. in 1840, 942. Its surface is irregular, but it is generally well cultivated.
BOXGROVE, a parish in Sussex, 3½ m. NE by E of Chichester. Area 3,410 acres. Pop. 736.
BOXHOLM, a town of Sweden, 25 m. WSW of Linkoping. In the environs is an iron mine, and on the banks of the Svarto are extensive iron works.
BOXLEY, a parish in Kent, 3 m. NNE of Maidstone. Area 5,670 acres. Pop. 1,398.
BOXMEER, a town of Holland, in the prov. of N Brabant, near the L bank of the Meuse, 28 m. ESE of Bois-le-Duc. Pop. 1,900. It contains a classical school.
BOXTED, a parish in Essex, 2 m. SE of Neyland, on the S bank of the Stour. Area 3,120 acres. Pop. 856.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 5 m. NE of Clare. Area 1,580 acres. Pop. 200.

BOXTEL, a town of Holland, in the prov. of N Brabant, arrond. and 8 m. S of Bois-le-Duc, on the r. bank of the Dommel. Pop. 2,600. A sanguinary battle between the French and Anglo-Dutch forces took place here in 1794.
BOXWELL, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. E by S of Wotton-under-Edge. Area 2,210 acres. Pop. 334.
BOXWORTH, a parish in Cambridgeshire, 6 m. NE by N of Caxton. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 326.
BOY (SAN), a town of Spain, in Catalonia, in the prov. and 5 m. WSW of Barcelona, on the r. bank of the Llobregat.

BOYACA, a department and town of New Grenada. The dep. comprises the 4 prov. of Pamplona, Casanare, Tunja, in which is the cap. of the same name, and Socorro. Pop. 444,000. The town is 8 m. SW of Tunja, and 60 m. NNE of Santa Fe-de- Bogota. The inhabitants are Indians; and their chief occupation is the making lime, with which they supply the surrounding district.
BOYADEL, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Silesia and regency of Liegnitz, circle and 14 m. E of Grüneberg, near the r. bank of the Oder. It has a fine castle. Pop. 1,550.
BOYANA. See BOIANA.
BOYANAGH, a parish in co. Galway, 63 m. ENE of Dunamore. Area 15,832 acres. Pop. 5,288.

BOYANNA, or BAYANA, a town of the island of Madagascar, on a bay of the same name, on the NW coast, in S lat. 16° 6', and E long. 45° 25'. Two rivers, each affording capacious harbours, discharge themselves into the bay.
BOYATT, a tything in the p. of Otterbourne, in Hants. Pop. 160.
BOYDAN, an islet about 1 m. in circumf., on the NE coast of Australia, in S lat. 11° 28', supposed to be that on which the crew of the Charles Eaton were massacred in 1834.
BOYDEN, or Lower Llangonovd, a hamlet in the p. of Llangonoyd, in Glamorganshire. Pop. 339.
BOYDEN, or BOIDEN, a town of Denmark, in the island of Fyen, and amt. of Vendborg, 6 m. E of Faaborg, on the Little Belt.
BOYDTON, a village, cap. of Mecklenburg co., in the state of Virginia, U.S., 109 m. SSW of Richmond.
BOYER, a commune of France, in the dep. of the Saone-et-Loire, cant. of Sennecy. Pop. 1,372. The environs afford considerable quantities of wine.

BOYER, a river of Lower Canada, in Lauzon seignory. It crosses the fiefs of Martiniere, Livaudiere, and Beaumont, and the seignories of St. Michel and St. Vallier, and flows into the St. Lawrence, nearly opposite St. Jean, in the island of Orleans.

BOYLE, a river of co. Roscommon, formed by the superfluent waters of Lough Gara, and by the numerous rivulets which flow into that lake. It runs 4 m. N of E from Lough Gara to the town of Boyle; 2 m. NE from Boyle to the river's expansion of Lough Key; and between 5 and 6 m. thence ESE to the Shannon, at a point 11 m. above Carrick-on- Shannon. From the bridge of Boyle to the Boathouse ford, 3 furl. above Lough Key, it falls 44 ft., and runs over a bed of rock, along a narrow valley; but from the Boat-house ford to the Shannon, it has little aggregate fall, and is so singularly expansive as to make the greater part of its course a chain of lakes. Alterations and artificial works upon it, proposed by the Commissioners for the Improvement of the Shannon navigation, will render it navigable for steam-boats to Boat-house ford, a distance of 9 m. from the Shannon.

BOYLE, a parish, containing a town of the same name, in co. Roscommon. Area 20,736 acres, of which 1,252 acres are water. Pop. in 1831, 12,597; in 1841, 12,591. The surface includes part of the Curlew mountains on the W, and part of Lough Key on the E; and possesses nearly every variety of character. The most interesting objects in the parish, additional to those in the town, are Lough Key and a portion of Lord Lorton's demesne of Rockingham. The water-area consists of 748 acres in Lough Key; 218 acres in Lough Gara; 151 acres in Lough Arrow; and 134 acres in small lakes. A principal height on the W border has an alt. of 863 ft.

BOYLE, a post, market, and corporate town, and formerly a parliamentary borough, in the p. described in the preceding article, on the Boyle river, on the road from Dublin to Sligo; 73 m. WNW of Carrickon-Shannon; 20 m. S by E of Sligo; and 84 m. NW of Dublin. The town consists of two divisions, the smaller and newer on the S bank of the river, and the larger and older on the N bank. Area 237 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,433; in 1841, 3,235. The old bridge, situated nearly in the middle of the town, consists of 5 low and rather narrow arches. The new bridge, situated 500 yards lower down, between the barrack and the abbey, consists of a single arch. The ruins of B. abbey are situated within an enclosure about 100 yards NNW of the new bridge, close to the new road to Sligo. The town is very fairly provided with shops, and is a considerable depot for the retail supply of the surrounding country.
BOYLE, a county of Kentucky, U.S., near the centre of the state, and comprising an area of 175 sq. m. It is drained by branches of the Salt and Dick rivers. The cap. is Danville.

BOYLSTON, a township of Worcester co., in the state of Massachusetts, U. S., 39 m. W of Boston, watered by the Nashna. Pop. in 1840, 797.—Also a township of Oswego co., New York, 140 m. NW of Albany. It is hilly, and is drained by Little Sandy and Trout creeks. Its soil, which consists of gravelly loam, is generally fertile. Pop. 481.
BOYLSTONE, a parish in Derbyshire, 7 m. S of Ashbourne. Area 1,360 acres. Pop. 343.
BOYNDIE, a parish in Banffshire, on the S side of the Moray frith. Area 3,400 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,122; in 1841, 1,501.

BOYNE, a chief and celebrated river of Ireland, in the prov. of Leinster. It rises in the bog of Allen, about 12 m. SE of Carbery, in co. Kildare; and drains that portion of the great bog which lies immediately north of the Grand canal. It runs about 4 m., chiefly W, to the boundary with King's co.; 4 m. NW and N between Kildare and King's co.; 6 m. NE between Kildare and Meath; about 32 m. chiefly NE, through Meath, cutting that BOY m. between Meath and Louth, to the Irish sea. Its chief affluents before it enters Meath, are the bogstreams called the Yellow and the Milltown; and its chief affluents in Meath, are the Deel from Westmeath, the Blackwater from Kildare, and the lower and greater Blackwater from Cavan. The river is crossed by the Royal canal soon after entering Meath, and washes the Meath towns of Trim, Navan, and Slane, and the Louth town of Drogheda. Over nearly the whole of its connection, either entire or partial, with Kildare, it is a sluggish and almost stagnant stream, and has rarely better scenery than that of a tame plain, and generally none other than a dreary expanse of morass; but, in its course through the rich champaign country of Meath, and between that county and Louth, it has a delightful variety of both motion and scenery. Tara-hill, towns, castles, monasteries, and battlefields on the river's margin, combine to associate its name with multitudinous historical recollections: and the abbeys of Clonard, Trim, Bective, Donaghmore, Slane, Mellifont, Monasterboice, and Drogheda on its banks, have freely sprinkled its name over monastic annals. The river is naturally navigable to Drogheda, 31 m. from the sea; and is affected by the tide to Oldbridge, 24 m. above Drogheda; but by artificial deepening, and by canal cuts, effected in 1770, the navigation was extended to Navan, a distance of 19 m. from the sea. The river has a total descent of 336 ft.; and drains an area of about 700 sq. m. The battle of the B., which annihilated the interests of James II., and rendered the cause of the Revolution triumphant, is known, not only throughout Ireland, but throughout Europe, and needs not to be minutely narrated. The action extended from the immediate vicinity of Drogheda on the E, to the bridge and village of Slane in the W, a distance of about 7 m.; but it was fought principally in the vicinity of Oldbridge and the hill of Donore, 24 m. W of Drogheda, and is there commemorated by a stone obelisk of about 150 ft. in height. James II., at the head of 27,000 Irish and French troops, and accompanied by the Dukes of Berwick and Tyrconnel, Generals Hamilton, Sarsfield, and Dorington, and Count Lauzin, lay encamped on the S side of the river; and Lord Iveagh occupied Drogheda, on his behalf, a little to the r., and commanded the main road to Dublin. James' army extended in two lines, with a difficult morass on their left, deep and dangerous fords in their front, and the village of Duleek, and an excellent pass for a retreating army in their rear. William, at the head of 30,000 English, French, Dutch, and Danes, and accompanied by the Duke of Schomberg, the earl of Schomberg, Generals Ginkell, Douglas, and Kirk, and many other distinguished persons, arrived on the S bank of the B., after his antagonists had fully settled themselves in their strong position. The grand movements of the battle commenced about 6 A. M., on the 1st of July, 1690. The right wing of William's army, commanded by Count Schomberg and General Douglas, marched toward the bridge of Slane, and eventually passed the ford below the bridge. Of the centre of the army, commanded by the Duke of Schomberg, the Dutch guards first entered the river at the ford of Oldbridge. While the conflict was in the most critical condition, William, accompanied by the Prince of Denmark, and commanding the left wing of the horse, consisting of English, Dutch, and Danes, passed at a ford, little more than a mile above Drogheda. James, throughout this eventful day, was stationed on the hill of Donore; and while the crown of the three kingdoms was the object of contest, stood amidst his guards rather as a spectator than as a general. Informed by those about him that he was in danger of being surrounded, James quitted Donore, and retired to Duleek, at the head of Sarsfield's regiment. His army followed, and effected a retreat, allowed by all to be admirably conducted, through the pass of Duleek. The loss of the Irish was said to have been 1,500, and that of William's army 500.
BOYNES, a commune and town of France, in the dep. of Loiret, cant. and 6 m. NE of Pithiviers. Pop. 1,822. The environs afford wax, honey, and saffron.
BOYNTON, a parish in the E. R. of Yorkshire, 21 m. W by N of Bridlington. Area 2,690 acres. Pop. 100.
BOYS (Rio Dos), a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Matto Grosso, which runs NNW, and unites with the Xingu, in S lat. 12° 20'.

BOYSTOWN, or BALTIBOYS, a parish in co. Wickgreat county into nearly equal parts and about 61 low. Area 25,134 acres. Pop. in 1831, 3,235; in 1841, 3,644. The surface is prevailingly mountainous, bold, and granitic; yet includes the greater part of the beautiful vale of the King's river. The summits of the Black-Rocks mountain and Moanbane in the interior, have altitudes of respectively 2,296 and 2,313 ft.; of Blackmoor-hill and Sorrel-hill, on the N boundary, respectively 1,464 and 1,915 ft.; of Mullaghcleevaun, Tonelagee, and another mountain on the E boundary, respectively 2,783, 2,301, and 2,408 ft.; and of Carrignagunneen, on the SE border, 1,782 ft. The King's river rises within the parish, and descends, while connected with it, from an elevation of 1,315 to one of 566 ft.; and the Ballinastocken, a tributary of the King's river, descends from an elevation of 1,495 ft.

BOYTON, a parish in Devonshire, 5 m. N by W of Launceston. Area 5,120 acres. Pop. 600.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 7 m. ESE of Woodbridge. Area 1,890 acres. Pop. 239.—Also a p. in Wilts, 6 m. SE by E of Warminster. Area 4,580 acres. Pop. 360.

BOYPEBA, or BOIPEBA, an island of Brazil, in the bay of Camamu, prov. of Bahia, and comarca of Ilheos, to the S of and separated by a narrow channel from the island of Tinhare. It comprises an area of about 6 sq. m.; and contains a town of the same name. Its chief production is rice.—There is a small town known as Boypeba-velha, on its W side.
BOZ, a commune of France, in the dep. of Aisne, cant. of Pont-de-Vaux. Pop. 866. It has a considerable commerce in cattle.

BOZA, a town of Transylvania, in the co. of Upper Weissemburg, 18 m. ESE of Kronstadt, at the entrance of the pass of the same name, one of the most frequented on the Walachian frontier. The river Boza, after descending the pass, makes a circuit to the SSE, and unites with the Busco in Lower Walachia.

BOZA, a town of New Grenada, in the prov. and 15 m. SW of Bogota, on the l. bank of the Payti. Pop. 1,200. It was formerly the occasional place of residence of the Spanish viceroys. The climate of the locality is cool and salubrious, and the soil fertile. Cattle are extensively reared in the environs.
BOZAWOLA, a small town of Poland, 22 m. SSW of Lublin.
BOZBERGA, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Argau, 7 m. SE of Lauffenburg.
BOZEAT, a parish in Northamptonshire, 6 m. S of Wellingborough. Area 2,400 acres. Pop. 845.
BOZEGOW, a privileged town of Bohemia, in the circle and 24 m. E of Tabor. It contains a castle.

BOZEL, or Bosel, a village of Savoy, cap. of a mandemento of the same name, in the prov. of Tarantaise, on the r. bank of the Doron, 6 m. SE of Moutiers, on one of the less-frequented roads across the Vanoise range to Mount Cenis. Pop. 1,500. It is situated in a populous valley, in which an extensive commerce is carried on in cattle, leather, skins, and cheese.
BOZIN. See Posing.

BOZINGEN, or BOUJEAN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. and 17 m. NNW of Bern, bailiwick of Nidau, and 13 m. NE of Bienne, near the Suse, which is here crossed by a stone bridge. It has considerable iron-works, wire-mills, &c.
BOZLINGEN. See BOTZLINGEN.
BOZOK, a town of Hungary, cap. of a marche in the comitat of Hont, 2 m. SSE of Karpen, and 13 m. SE of Schemnitz. The locality is filled with trachytic deposits.
BOZOULS, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Aveyron, arrond. of Rodez. The cant. comprises 5 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 7,149. The vil. is 15 m. from Rodez. Pop. 2,876.
BOZRA. See Bosrah. I. BRA
BOZRAH, a township of New London co., in the state of Connecticut, U. S., 33 m. ESE of Hartford. It is watered by the Gantic, and is generally fertile. Pop. in 1840, 1,067.
BOZSUR, a town of Austria, in the Banat, on a tributary of the Temes, 40 m. ENE of Temesvar.

BOZZOLO, a town of Venetian Lombardy, cap. of a dist. of the same name, in the prov. and 16 m. WSW of Mantua. Pop. 4,000. It is pleasantly situated near the r. bank of the Oglio, and is defended by ancient fortifications. It has a weekly market, and a fair is held once a-year.

BRA, a town of Piedmont, cap. of a mandemento of the same name, in the prov. and 10 m. W of Alba, near the l. bank of the Stura, 25 m. NNE of Coni. Pop. 8,000. It contains a communal college and 3 parish churches, and has an extensive commerce in silk, cattle, grain and wine. The environs produce silk in great abundance and of excellent quality.
BRAA. See BRAHE.

BRAAKE, or Brake, a town of Oldenburg, in the duchy and 25 m. NNE of Oldenburg, circle of Ovelgonne, on the l. bank of the Weser. Pop. 1,315. It has extensive building-docks, and is one of the principal maritime commercial entrepots in the duchy.
BRABA POINT, a promontory of the island of Cuba, on the N coast, in N lat. 21° 48', and W long. 77° 50'.

BRABANT (NORTH), a province of Holland; bounded on the N by the Maas or Meuse, which separates it from the prov. of S Holland, and from Gelderland; on the W by Limburg, from which also it is separated over a distance of about 12 m. by the Maas; on the S by the Belgian frontier of Limburg and Antwerp; and on the W by the Eendraght channel, connecting the S arm of the Maas with the Hond or W Scheldt, which separates it from the Dutch prov. of Zealand. Area, according to the Almanach de Gotha, 1,983 sq. m.; but in Macgregor's Commercial Tariffs, and in the Penny Cyclopedia, it is stated at only 1,653 sq. m. Pop. in 1824, 332,551; in 1844, 382,709; on 1st January, 1848, 403,723. It lies between 51° 12' and 51° 45' N lat.; and 4° 10' and 5° 55' E long. Its greatest length from E to W is about 80 geog. m.; its greatest breadth, 35 m. Its surface is in general level, with a uniform inclination towards the N. A considerable proportion of the surface is covered with pine-forests; and there are extensive marshes and peaty districts .- The principal rivers are all affluents of the Maas; and, in succession from W to E, are the Rozendaal, the Mark or Merk, the Donge, the Ley, the Dommel, and its affluent the Aa. The Zind Willems Vaart canal, running from Bois-le-Duc to Maestricht, crosses the eastern part of the prov. in a line generally a little to the S of the Aa river, and passes through the Peel-canal or marsh, on the SE frontier, to the E of Eindhoven.—This prov. contains a large proportion of waste land, especially on its S frontiers, where there is almost no alluvial soil, and the dry sands are impregnated with iron. A few spots, however, of rich soil occur, on which good crops of wheat, flax, colza, potatoes, and tobacco are raised. Estimating the superficies at 511,673 Dutch bunders, [1 bunder = 2.4736 acres,] the quantity of cultivated land in 1833 was 296,811 bunders; of heaths, reed and rush lands, downs, bogs, &c., 179,384 bunders; and of water surface, including rivers, lakes, canals, ponds, and morasses, 22,262 bunders. Cattle and sheep are extensively reared.—The prov. is divided into the three arrondissements of Breda, Eindhoven, and Bois-le-Duc.—The chief towns are the three just named, Bergen-op-Zoom, Oosterhout, and Tilburg. -The pop. is chiefly Catholic. On the 1st of Jan., 1830, out of a pop. of 349,700, the number of Catho- 3 K lics was 305,445; of Protestants, 41,840; of Jews, 1,476.—The pop. returned at that date as residing in towns was 70,949; in rural districts, 277,942. The manufacturing industry of the prov. embraces cotton, linen, and woollen fabrics, leather, pottery-ware, brandy, and gin.—The history of this province is sketched in the historical notice of the duchy of Brabant, which follows the next article.

BRABANT (South), a province of Belgium; bounded on the N by the prov. of Antwerp; on the E by Limburg and Liege; on the S by Namur and Hainault; and on the W by Hainault and Flanders. Area, according to the Almanach de Gotha, 1,270 sq. m. Pop. in 1830, 556,146; in 1840, 613,687; in 1846, 690,549. It lies between 50° 30' and 51° 5' N lat.; and 4° and 5° 10' E long.—The surface throughout is level, diversified only in the E and S by a few small hills, the steep banks of the rivers, and some forests. The soil is on the whole very fertile, a great part being rich mould; the best soil lies towards the W frontiers. Sand, with decayed shells, is predominant in many places.—The principal rivers are the Senne, the Dyle, and the Demer. The two former intersect the prov. from S to N; the Demer enters it on the E, and flowing W, near the N frontier, enters the prov. of Antwerp a little above Mechlin.—Two lines of railroad intersect the prov.; one running SSW from Mechlin to Brussels, and thence, by Hal, to the French frontier; the other running from Mechlin SE, by Louvain and Tirlemont, to Liege. The two large canals of Louvain and Brussels likewise facilitate the communication.—The climate is less damp than that of N Brabant, and is considered healthy. The prevailing winds blow from the S and W. The productions are the common domestic animals, fowls, game, fish, corn of all kinds, vegetables, potatoes, flax, oilplants, hops, honey, fruit, and wood. In warm summers maize ripens well. The whole country is in excellent cultivation, and seems a garden. The rich pastures favour the rearing of cattle, which are of a large breed. There are some considerable forests, of which that of Soignies to the S of Brussels is the largest. The lace-manufactures of this prov. are highly celebrated; there are also some cotton and linen manufactories, sugar and salt-refineries, distilleries, breweries, soap-works, and chemical works. The number of steam-engines, within the prov., in July 1840, was 103, equivalent to a power of 2,856 horses.—The pop. is mostly Catholic. In the N districts they speak Flemish, and in the S, Walloon; but French is also very generally spoken, particularly in the towns.—The prov. is divided into 3 arrondissements, viz., Brussels, Louvain, and Nivelles, which in 1841 were subdivided into 23 cantons, and 326 rural and 8 town communes.—The chief towns are Brussels, Aerschot, Drest, Hal, Louvain, Nivelles, Tirlemont, and Wavre.—In 1840 this prov. was represented in the national chambers by 7 senators, and 14 representatives, who were chosen by 7,379 electors. The number of primary schools in 1839 was 744, with 67,186 scholars.

BRABANT (Duchy of), an ancient division of Western Europe, lying between the Meuse and the Scheldt, and of which the N and S portions have been described in the two preceding articles. This territory first appears in history under the name of the Pagus Brachatensis. B. has always held pre-eminence among the provinces of the Low Countries. It was anciently the seat of government and the residence of the sovereign; and in the general assembly of the states, the first place and voice belonged to the deputies of B. This prov. was the original residence of the family of Charlemagne, and was first erected into a duchy by that monarch in the beginning of the 9th cent. It continued to be governed, as a separate state, by princes of his family until 1005, when it devolved to Lambert II., Count of Louvain, by his marriage with the sister and heiress of Otto, the last duke of B. After remaining three centuries under the house of Louvain, B. in 1404 descended, by the female line, to the dukes of Burgundy, and united to the other possessions of these princes formed one of the richest domains in Europe. Even so early as the 9th cent. BRA the constitution of B. affords an example of civil liberty scarcely surpassed at this day by any nation in the world. The Blyde Inkomste van Brabant, or 'Joyons entry of B.,' the great charter of their freedom, was so named because the sovereign, when he entered upon his government, bound himself by an oath to govern according to this charter. In it the conditions are declared on which the people consented to yield obedience, and on which the prince was willing to reign; and a remarkable clause is added, that if the sovereign should infringe any article of the 'Joyous Entry,' his subjects should ipse facto be released from all duty and service until due reparation should be made. By this instrument, no arbitrary mandate could deprive a citizen of his liberty; his dwelling was sacred; and if suspected of a crime, the officers of justice could not enter his house unless two magistrates were present; and when apprehended, he had a right, after a limited time, to call upon his judges to determine whether there were sufficient grounds for his detention. Without the consent and authority of the states,—which consisted of three orders, the clergy, the nobles, and the commons, -no taxes could be imposed, or subsidies granted; and in the latter case the commons could not give their votes until they had collected the sense of their constituents in the different cities which they represented. The dukes of B. and Burgundy, and in later times the princes of Austria, governed according to this charter, fortified by what was called 'the Golden bull of B.,' by which it was forbidden to all the princes of the empire, within or without their dominions, to exercise any jurisdiction over natives of B., unless justice should be denied by their own sovereign. Under the government of Philip the Good, B. rose to opulence and distinction: its fairs were crowded with merchants from every nation, and its manufactures of woollen and linen cloths were in great demand over all Europe. Philip de Commines likened the prosperity of this country to the plenty of the land-of-promise. About the end of the 15th cent., the sovereignty of B., with the other provinces of the Netherlands, was transferred to the house of Austria, by the marriage of Mary of Burgundy with the Arch-duke Maximilian, son and successor of Frederick IV. But neither its privileges nor its prosperity were affected by the change; and it continued to equal if not surpass its ancient grandeur under the dukes of Burgundy. Antwerp had become the great magazine of the northern nations; the Scheldt was covered with its numerous fleets; and, according to Guicciardini, it seemed to be the common city of all nations, for all languages were spoken in its mart. But upon the abdication of Charles V., B. came into the possession of Philip II., and its disasters and fall commenced. Philip deluged it with blood, and despoiled its citizens of those privileges which so many princes had respected. Under his successors, commerce rapidly declined; its deserted cities retained only, in their wide extent, the evidences of their former prosperity; and the people, dispirited by oppression, appeared to have lost even the remembrance of their ancient greatness. The numerous branches of the Scheldt were seized by the states of Holland, now an independent union, and triumphant in war; and that power likewise extorted the humiliating concession that Antwerp, whose competition the Dutch dreaded, should hold no communication with the ocean by the navigation of that river. In the 17th cent, the United Provinces took possession of the northern part of this duchy, which they called Dutch Brabant, and which formed an eighth state of the republic; and after the battle of Ramilies in 1706, the remaining part was ceded by Spain to the house of Austria, in the treaty of Utrecht. During the last century, under the auspices of the Austrian emperors, a commercial spirit began to revive, and an attempt was made to animate this and the neighbouring provinces, known as the Austrian Netherlands, by opening a trade to the East Indies; but though the insurrections occasioned by the arbitrary but wise measures of the emperor Joseph II, promised them liberty and independence, yet the revolution in which they terminated only left them under the more intolerable despotism of an absolute aristocracy. When the French passed the Rhine in the Revolutionary war, they established themselves in this country; and by the treaty of Campo Formio in 1797, and that of Luneville in 1801, Austrian B. was ceded to France, when it was formed into the departments of the Deux Nethes, and the Dyle; and on the annexation of Holland to the French empire, Dutch B. was converted into the department of the Scheldt. At the congress of Vienna, both North and South B., with the other provinces of Holland and the Netherlands, were erected into the kingdom of the Netherlands; but in 1830, South B. shared the fortunes of the other revolting provinces; and has since formed an important part of the kingdom of Belgium.

BRABEESH, or Zawat, a district of W Africa, on the S skirts of the Sahara, to the N of Timbuctu. Caillie, who crossed this country in his route from Timbuctu to Marocco, describes the country as covered with forests of date-trees, under which the inhabitants cultivate wheat, barley, and vegetables. The soil is a fine sand, upon a harder deposit mixed with small black and yellow pebbles, and resting at the depth of about 25 ft. on rocks which appeared to Caillie to be granitic.
BRABOURNE, a parish in Kent, 5 m. E of Ashford. Area 3,640 acres. Pop. 889. It comprises the hamlets of East B. and West B.

BRACADALE, a parish in the island of Skye, in Inverness-shire, about 17 m. in length, and from 4 to 7 m. in breadth. It is much intersected by arms of the sea. The surface, in general, is hilly, with some level spots adjacent to the sea; the shore is for the most part high and rocky. The principal bays or harbours are: Loch-Bracadale, a good and safe harbour; Loch-Harport, a considerable branch of Loch- Bracadale, where vessels may ride with safety; and Loch-Eynort, 7 m. S of Loch-Bracadale. To the S of Eynort, at the distance of 3 m., is Loch-Brettle, an open bay, and not a safe harbour. The islands in this parish are Haversay, Vuiay, Oransay, and Souay. A considerable ridge of lofty hills runs betwixt this p. and that of Strath. They are commonly called the Culinn or Coolin hills. Pop. in 1801, 1,865; in 1831, 1,769; in 1841, 1,824.—The natives of Skye were celebrated for the second sight; and B., of all its wild districts, appears to have been that in which the supernatural gift was most frequent and potent in its manifestations.
BRACARA. See BRAGA.

BRACCIANO, a town of the States-of-the-Church, in the comarca and 19 m. NW of Rome, on the SW side of the lake of the same name. Pop. 1,800. It contains a fine baronial castle of the 15th cent., belonging to the dukes of Bracciano, and has a manufactory of paper. In the environs are thermal baths. The lake of B., the Sabatina Stagna or Lacus Sabatinus of the Romans, is 22 m. in circumf., and at an alt. of 2,438 ft. above sea-level. It discharges itself by the Arrone, into the Mediterranean.
BRACCO, a town of Genoa, in the district and 10 m. ESE of Chiavari, and 33 m. ESE of Genoa.
BRACEBOROUGH, a parish in Lincolnshire, 55 m. WNW of Market-Deeping. Area 2,230 acres. Pop. 231.
BRACEBRIDGE, a parish in Lincolnshire, in the liberty and 3 m. S by E of Lincoln. Pop. 127.
BRACEBY, a parish in Lincolnshire, 5 m. W by N of Folkingham. Area 970 acres. Pop. 155.
BRACE-MEOL, a parish in Salop, in the liberty and 2 m. S of Shrewsbury. Pop. 1,195.
BRACEVILLE, a township of Trumbull co., U.S., in the state of Ohio, 164 m. NE of Columbus. It affords excellent pasturage, and has extensive dairies.
BRACEWELL, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 9 m. W by S of Skipton. Area 1,920 acres. Pop. 153.
BRACHELEN, or Brakel, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of the Rhine, regency of Aix-la-Chapelle, 9 m. NW of Juliers, near the l. bank of the Roer. Pop. 2,200. It contains extensive paper-mills.
BRACHSTADT, a town of Prussian Saxony, in the circle of Merseburg, NE of Halle. Pop. 497.

BRACHT, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Cleves Berg, regency and 40 m. SW of Cleves. Pop. 725. It is situated in the midst of an uncultivated plain, but is well-built, and contains several Catholic and Calvinistic churches. It has extensive manufactories of linen and pottery, besides bleacheries, breweries, and oil-mills.
BRACHWITZ, a town of Prussian Saxony, in the circle of Merseburg, NW of Halle. Pop. 312.

BRACIEUX, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Loir-et-Cher, arrond. of Blois.-The cant. comprises 14 com., and contained in 1831 a pop. of 11,067.—The village is 12 m. SE of Blois. Pop. 930.
BRACKE, a town of Sweden, in the amt and 43 m. SE of Ostersund.

BRACKEL, or BRAKEL, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, in the regency and 41 m. SSE of Minden, on the Brucht, a tributary of the Nette. | a course of 52 m., into the gulf of Tarento, 23 m. BRA Pop. in 1837, 2,508. It has considerable breweries, distilleries, and vinegar manufactories. The environs produce lint in great quantity.

BRACKEN, a county in the state of Kentucky, U. S., on the Ohio river. It comprises an area of 220 sq. m. It is drained in the S by the Licking; and is generally fertile and productive. The cap. is Augusta. Pop. in 1830, 6,518; in 1840, 7,053.
BRACKENBERG, a village and bailiwick of Hanover, in the principality and 11 m. SW of Gottingen, and 3 m. N of Hedemunden. Pop. of bail. 1,109.
BRACKENFIELD, a chapelry in the p. of Morton, in Derbyshire, 4 m. NW of Alfreton. Pop. 459.

BRACKENHEIM, a town and bailiwick of Wurtemberg, in the circle of the Neckar, 22 m. NNW of Stuttgart, near the l. bank of the Tabor. Pop. of town 1,670. In the environs is the castle of Magenheim, belonging to the former lords of B.—The bailiwick comprises an area of 36 sq. m., and a pop. of 21,924. It is to a great extent covered with vineyards.
BRACKENTHWAITE, a township in the chapelry of Lorton, Cumberland, 8 m. W of Keswick. Pop. 116.

BRACKLEY, a borough, market-town, and parish, in the co. of Northampton; 63 m. NW of London; 93 m. E by S of Banbury; and 11 m. SW of Towcester. The town stands on a rising ground, near a branch of the river Ouse, by which it is separated from Buckinghamshire. It consists mainly of a single street, nearly a mile in length, containing some good houses, chiefly built of stone. Formerly this town was a great mart for wool; but the inhabitants are now chiefly employed in the manufacture of bobbin-lace, and boots and shoes. The town is divided into New and Old Brackley. The limits of the borough are co-extensive with those of the parish. The elective franchise was first conferred in the first of Edward VI.; and two members were returned by the corporation, in number only 33, until the Reform act disfranchised this borough. The church is an ancient building, with a lofty square tower, and contains a curious Norman font.—The B. poor-law union comprehends 30 parishes, embracing an area of 87 sq. m.; with a pop., in 1831, of 13,351; in 1841, of 13,508. The average annual expenditure on the poor of this district, during the three years preceding the formation of the union, was £11,743. Expenditure, in 1838, £6,301; in 1846, £7,202.

BRACKLIN, a bog on the border of the counties of Westmeath and Meath, 13 m. NE of Rathfarn. Its length, from near Islandford bridge to Coxtown, is 4½ m.; its breadth, from Bracklin to Carranstown, 3½ m.; and its area 9,634 acres. Its summit occurs near Bracklin, and lies 41 ft. above the level of the Deel, and <math>279\frac{3}{4}</math> ft. above that of high-water in Dublin bay.

BRACKWEDE, a village of Prussia, in the prov. of Westphalia, regency of Minden, circle and 3 m. S of Bielfeld. Pop. 1,094. It has an extensive spinning mill, a manufactory of linen, and a copper foundery. Its trade consists chiefly in butter.
BRACLAW. See Bratslaf.
BRACMA, or Bragmans Point, a promontory of Guatemala, on the Mosquito coast, in N lat. 13° 55', and W long. 82° 54′, 70 m. S of Cape Gracias a Dios.
BRACO. See ARDOCH.
BRACON-ASH, a parish in Norfolk, 7 m. S by W of Norwich. Area 950 acres. Pop. 293.
BRAD, a town of Transylvania, cap. of a marche of the same name, in the comitat of Tarand, on the r. bank of the Feherkoros, 35 m. WNW of Karlsburg.

BRADANO, or Brandano, a river of Naples, in the prov. of Basilicato, which takes its rise on the E base of Mount Caruso, runs to the SE, and falls, after WSW of the town of that name. This river was the Bradanus of the Romans, celebrated as the scene of the reconciliation of Marc Antony and Octavius.

BRADBORNE, a parish in Derbyshire, 5 m. NNE of Ashborne, comprising the chapelries of Atlow, Ballidon, and Brassington, the townships of Aldwark and B., and the hamlet of Leahall. Area 6,400 acres. Pop. 1,303.
BRADBURY, a township in the p. of Sedgefield, co.-palatine of Durham, 101 m. S by E of Durham. Area 1,970 acres. Pop. 167.
BRADDAN, a parish in the isle of Man. Pop. 2,379.
BRADDEN, a parish in the co. of Northampton, 3 m. W of Towcester. Area 1,000 acres. Pop. 171.
BRADENHAM, a parish in Buckinghamshire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. NW by N of High Wycombe. Area 830 acres. Pop. 226.
BRADENHAM (EAST), a parish in Norfolk, 5 m. SW of East Dereham. Area 2,120 acres. Pop. 368.
BRADENHAM (West), a parish in Norfolk, 5½ m. SW by W of East Dereham. Area 1,790 acres. Pop. 364.
BRADERUP, a town of Denmark, in the prov. of Sleswick, 7 m. S of Tonder.
BRADESTON, a parish in Norfolk, 4 m. WSW of Acle. Area 630 acres. Pop. 126.
BRADFIELD, a parish in Berks, 8 m. W of Reading. Area 4,270 acres. Pop. 1,042.
BRADFIELD, a parish in Essex, 3 m. ESE of Maningtree, on the S bank of the navigable river Stour. Area 2,270 acres. Pop. 995.
BRADFIELD, a parish in Norfolk, 21 m. NNW of North Walsham. Area 630 acres. Pop. 185.
BRADFIELD, a chapelry in the p. of Ecclesfield, W. R. of Yorkshire, 7 m. NW by N of Sheffield.
BRADFIELD-COMBUST, a parish in Suffolk, 5½ m. S by E of Bury-St.-Edmund's. Area 590 acres. Pop. 192.
BRADFIELD (St. Clare), a parish in Suffolk, 5 m. SE of Bury-St.-Edmund's. Area 1,190 acres. Pop. 240.

BRADFIELD (St. George), a parish in Suffolk, 4½ m. SE of Bury-St.-Edmund's, intersected by the Ipswich and St.-Edmund's-Bury railway, which here passes through a tunnel 1 m. in length. Area 2,090 acres. Pop. 479.

BRADFORD, a parish in Devon, 8 m. W by N of Hatherleigh, on the S bank of the Torridge. Area 3,670 acres. Pop. 530.—Also a township in the p. of Manchester, Lancashire, 2 m. E of Manchester. Area 260 acres. Pop. 911. It is within the boundary of the parliamentary borough of Manchester .- Also a township in the p. of Bambrough, Northumberland, 23 m. SE of Belford. Pop. 34.—Also a township in the p. of Bolam, Northumberland, 15 m. NW of Newcastle-upon-Tyne. Pop. 38.—Also a parish in the co. of Somerset, 3 m. WSW of Taunton, intersected by the Bristol and Exeter railway. Area 1,710 acres. Pop. 550.

BRADFORD, a market-town and parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire; 196 m. NNW of London; 35 m. SW of York; and 13 m. NW of Wakefield; intersected by the Bradford canal. The p. is 15 m. in length; and, at an average, 4 m. in breadth; and comprises the market-town of the same name, the chapelries of Haworth, Horton, and Thornton, and the townships of Allerton, North Bierley, Bowling, Clayton, Eccleshill, Heaton, Manningham, Shipley, and Wilsden. Area of p. 33,710 acres. Pop. in 1801 29,794; in 1831, 76,996; in 1841, 105,257. Assessed property in 1815, £76,773; in 1842-3, £225,261.— The town, which is built almost entirely of fine freestone, receives its name from its vicinity to an ancient ford over a small stream tributary to the river BRA Aire. It stands in a beautiful situation at the junction of three fine valleys, forming nearly a central point from the different towns of Halifax, Keighley, Leeds, Wakefield, Dewsbury, and Huddersfield. The piece-hall, for the exhibition and sale of stuff-goods, is a spacious building, 144 ft. long, by 36 ft. broad, consisting of two stories. Here are also a good market, with a spacious enclosed area; and an elegant exchange, in which there are a news-room and library. B. owes much to its situation in the very centre of the great manufacturing districts of Yorkshire, and to the abundance of coal in the vicinity. Ironstone is also plentiful, and there are some extensive foundries. The leading manufacture of B. is of worsted stuffs, here called "merinos" and "pieces." Worsted yarn is also spun extensively in the town; and B. is the great wool-market of Great Britain. The manufactures are now almost entirely confined to the products of machinery. In 1838 there were 2 cotton, 9 woollen, and 142 worsted mills, employing collectively 11,675 hands, within the parish. Commercial operations have been greatly facilitated by a branch from the Leeds and Liverpool canal, near Shipley, which has been led into the very centre of the town, and which connects it with the German ocean on the one hand, and the Irish sea on the other. The Leeds railway, 133 m. in length, will also greatly promote the prosperity of this enterprising town. This line commences at Leeds, within three minutes' walk of the court-house, commercialbuildings, and the post-office; passes along the valley of the Aire, through the centre of the clothing district, comprising Armley, Bramley, Kirkstall, Horsforth, Calverley, Rawden, Yeadon, Guiseley, Apperley-bridge, Eccleshill, Idle, Baildon, and Shipley; and terminates at B., within 300 yards of the exchange, piece-hall, and principal streets of the town. B. is also connected with Skipton by a railway, 18 m. in length, passing through Kildwick, Keighley, Bingley, and Shipley. The rapidity with which the pop. of B. has increased, affords one of the best evidences of its prosperity. Leland thus describes it in the reign of Henry VIII.—"Bradeford, a prety quick market toune dimidio aut eo amplius minus Wackefelda. It hath one paroche church, and a chapel of St. Sitha. It standeth much by clothing." Bradford, including the townships of Manningham and Bowling, and the chapelries of Little and Great Horton, now returns two members to parliament. Pop. of parl. borough in 1841, 66,508. Number of electors in 1842-3, 1,722. It is also one of the polling-places for the members for the west riding of Yorkshire. A mechanic's institute has been established here, for which a commodious building has been recently erected. Airedale college, in the vicinity of the town, is a valuable and flourishing institution for the education of young men for the ministry in the Congregational denomination; the Baptists have a similar institution at Horton; and the Wesleyan Methodists have a school for the education of ministers' sons at Woodhouse grove. Pop. of the township in 1801, 6,393; in 1831, 23,233; in 1841, 34,560. Area 1,680 acres. The Bradford poor-law union, embracing an area of 61 sq. m., with a pop. returned in 1831 at 94,621, and in 1841 at 132,164, expended £20,106 in 1841, and £19,498 in 1846, for the relief and maintenance of the poor.

BRADFORD, a township of Steuben co., in the state of New York, U. S., 204 m. WSW of Albany. It is hilly, but possesses a fertile soil. Pop. in 1840, 1,547.—Also a township of Penobscot co., in the state of Maine, 94 m. NE of Augustus. Pop. 1,000. -Also a township of Merrimac co., in the state of New Hampshire, 25 m. W of Concord, midway between the Merrimac and Connecticut rivers. It contains several large ponds. Pop. 1,331.—Also a township of Orange co., in the state of Vermont, on the Connecticut, 32 m. SE of Montpelier. It contains a neatly-built village near the mouth of Waits river, in which globes were first manufactured in the U. S. Pop. of t. 1,655.—Also a township of Essex co., in the state of Massachusetts, on the Merrimac, opposite Haverhill, with which it is connected by a bridge 650 ft. in length, and 35 m. N of Boston. It is hilly, but fertile, and Johnson's creek, with which it is intersected, affords good water-power. Pop. 2,222.—Also a county of the state of Pennsylvania, comprising an area of 1,200 sq. m., and watered by the N branch of the Susquehanna and the Chemung rivers, and the Towanda and Sugar creeks. It is generally mountainous, but the soil, especially near the Susquehanna, is to a great extent rich and productive. Bituminous coal is found near Towanda creek. Cap., Towanda. Pop. in 1830, 19,669; in 1840, 32,769.—Also a township of M'Kean co., in the state of Pennsylvania. Pop. 375.—Also a township of Lincoln co., in the state of Missouri. Pop. 1,319.
BRADFORD-ABBAS, a parish in Dorsetshire, 4 m. SW of Sherborne, intersected by the South Western railway. Area 1,810 acres. Pop. 652.

BRADFORD (GREAT), a parish in Wilts, 100 m. W of London; 31 m. NW of Salisbury; intersected by the river Avon, and the Kennet and Avon canal. It comprises the market-town of the same name, the chapelries of Atworth, Holt, Winsley with Limpley- Stoke, and South Wraxhall, and the tythings of Leigh - Wooley and Trowle. Area 11,740 acres. Pop. 10,563. The church is an ancient and handsome edifice. The town is finely situated on the Lower Avon, by which it is divided into two portions,—that on the N being called the Old, and that on the S, the New town. The old town is built at various degrees of elevation, on a rocky ridge rising abruptly from the river's bank, and presents a very picturesque appearance. The river is crossed by two bridges,—a very ancient one of 9 arches, and a more modern one of 4. The pop. is chiefly employed in the manufacture of woollen cloth. Leland mentions these manufactures as flourishing here in the reign of Henry VIII. The finer cloths are made from Spanish and Saxony wool, and it is said that the river is peculiarly favourable to the dyeing of them. In 1838 there were 4 woollen mills, employing 418 hands, within this parish.
BRADFORD-PEVERELL, a parish in Dorsetshire, 3 m. NW by N of Dorchester. Area 2,700 acres. Pop. 355.
BRADFORD (WEST), a township in the p. of Mitton, W. R. of Yorkshire, 2 m. N of Clitheroe. Pop. in 1821, 564; in 1841, 366.

BRADING, a parish in the Isle of Wight, 7 m. E by S of Newport, and 95 m. SE of London. It includes the borough of B. The town stands at the head of an inlet called Brading-haven, which is a marshy tract, of nearly 900 acres in extent, covered by the tide to a considerable depth, so that brigs of a moderate size can enter the harbour and approach the town. Attempts have been made, but hitherto without success, to exclude the sea from the area of the harbour. The church is a very ancient structure. Area of p. 7,350 acres. Pop. in 1801, 1,529; in 1831, 2,227; in 1841, 2,701.

BRADLEY, a township in the p. of Malpas, in Cheshire, 2 m. SE of Malpas. Area 940 acres. Pop. 99.—Also a parish in Derbyshire, 2½ m. E of Ashborne. Area 1,860 acres. Pop. 271.—Also a parish in Lincolnshire, 21 m. SW of Great Grimsby. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 106.—Also a parish in the co. of Southampton, 6 m. WNW of Alton. Area 670 acres. Pop. 125.—Also a parish in Stafford- BRA shire, 3½ m. NW of Penkridge. Area 4,690 acres. Pop. 649.
BRADLEY (GREAT), a parish in Suffolk, 8 m. NW of Clare. Area 2,280 acres. Pop. 544.
